attributes.stem:
A 29-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of white patches developing on his face, particularly around his eyes and mouth. The patches are completely depigmented and have well-defined borders. He is distressed about the appearance and reports that the patches are slowly enlarging. He thought it was due to a new sunscreen initially but had ceased using the sunscreen after the first month. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. There is no family history of autoimmune conditions.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are symmetrical, well-demarcated depigmented patches around his eyes and mouth. Wood's lamp examination confirms complete depigmentation. No other body areas are affected.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a typical case of vitiligo affecting the face. The characteristic features include well-demarcated, chalky-white completely depigmented patches with a predilection for periorificial areas. The condition results from autoimmune destruction of melanocytes, and affects 0.5 - 2% of people. It can be triggered by environmental factors or stress, and seems to affect males and females equally with some genetic component. It typically presents in the early 20s however can also present at any age. The affects are predominately cosmetic and the psychological impact of vitiligo on patients should not be ignored. The patients are also at high risk of other autoimmune conditions. Treatment is difficult.

Topical tacrolimus is the most appropriate first-line treatment for facial vitiligo. As a calcineurin inhibitor, it suppresses the local immune response that leads to melanocyte destruction. It is particularly effective for facial lesions and groin lesions, and has fewer side effects compared to topical corticosteroids, which can cause skin atrophy.

Narrow-band ultraviolet B phototherapy is an effective treatment for vitiligo but is typically reserved for widespread disease affecting multiple body sites. It would be excessive for localised facial involvement and not for Fitzpatrick skin type 1, and requires multiple hospital visits over several months.

Oral corticosteroids are occasionally used for rapidly progressive vitiligo but carry significant systemic side effects. They are not appropriate for stable, localised disease such as in this case.

Topical calcipotriol, a vitamin D analogue, has shown some benefit in vitiligo when combined with other treatments but is not recommended as monotherapy. It is less effective than tacrolimus for facial lesions.

Surgical melanocyte transplantation techniques such as split-thickness grafting or cellular grafting are reserved for stable vitiligo that has been unresponsive to medical therapy for at least 12 months. They are not first-line treatments.

Management should also include photoprotection advice, camouflage options if desired, and psychological support as vitiligo can have significant impacts on quality of life and self-esteem.
learningPoints: Topical tacrolimus is first-line therapy for facial vitiligo.
Psychological support is an essential component of vitiligo management.
references: Joge RR, Kathane PU, Joshi SH. Vitiligo: A Narrative Review. Cureus. 2022.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9586189/

Alk J et al.  Estimating the burder of vitiligo: a systematic review and modelling study.  The Lancet Public Health. 2024: 9 (6).  Available at: https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanpub/article/PIIS2468-2667(24)00026-4/fulltext
Aslayaydeh B, Amman J, Oakley A. Vitiligo. DermNet NZ.  2022.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/vitiligo
